## vvx
Job Jockey Kit - MCP tools for project initiative management

attachment.content:
- cma-prep-pr: Prepare branch for upstream PR contribution
- rbk-prep-release: Prepare release candidate for upstream delivery
- rbk-wrangle: Muster, then roster RBK and JJK heats as two space-padded plain-text blocks
- vvc-BREAK-LOCK: Force-break a stuck VVC lock after a crash.
- vvc-commit: Guarded git commit
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Docket:
  ## Character
  Design exploration — judgment, not mechanical. Output is a recommendation, not an implementation.
  
  Evaluate refactoring RBRA from one-file-per-role-type (director, retriever — each with its
  own cult verbs) to one-file-per-person carrying any combination of roles. Collapses the
  role-typed verb surface and aligns the keyfile tier with the federation model's
  principal -> capability-set shape (smoother eventual migration).
  
  The lifecycle split is its enabler: once identity (establish) and credential (re-key) are
  separated, "a principal with a role-set" is the natural next shape.
  
  Key question to resolve: does per-person multi-role preserve least-privilege? Today's
  role-typed SAs enforce it by construction — a retriever cannot act as director. A per-person
  SA is only as least-privilege as the grants chosen; show the security guarantee survives,
  not just the convenience.
  
  Source: the federation footnote in `Memos/memo-20260604-credential-churn-leak-and-propagation-races.md`;
  `Memos/memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`.
  
  Done: a decision/recommendation (do / don't / how), not implementation.

1	# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)
2	
3	*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
4	crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
5	repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
6	canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*
7	
8	## Headline
9	
10	The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
11	an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
12	lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
13	The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
14	separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
15	hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.
16	
17	## Objects
18	
19	- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
20	  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
21	- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
22	  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
23	- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
24	  a **Resource**.
25	- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
26	  repo**, the **Mason SA**.
27	
28	## Three independent lifecycles
29	
30	1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
31	   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
32	2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
33	   credential; the SA is untouched.*
34	3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
35	   by member = SA.
36	
37	Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
38	kill the old one.
39	
40	## The hole
41	
42	The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
43	**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
44	does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:
45	
46	- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
47	  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
48	  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
49	  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
50	- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
51	  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
52	  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
53	  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.
54	
55	## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)
56	
57	- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
58	  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
59	  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
60	  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
61	  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
62	  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
63	  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
64	- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
65	  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
66	  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
67	  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
68	  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
69	  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
70	  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
71	  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
72	- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
73	  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
74	  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
75	  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
76	  a not-yet-converged view.
77	
78	Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
79	routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
80	crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.
81	
82	## The fix — separate the lifecycles
83	
84	Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:
85	
86	- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
87	  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
88	  already established.
89	- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
90	  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
91	  Bindings.
92	- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
93	  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
94	  rather than every cycle.
95	
96	This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
97	and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:
98	
99	1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
100	   teardown).
101	2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
102	3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
103	4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
104	5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
105	   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
106	   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
107	6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
108	   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
109	   shows the policy is writable).
110	7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
111	   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
112	8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
113	   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.
114	
115	## Footnote: federation
116	
117	The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
118	grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
119	no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
120	**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
121	repaired in place as above.
122	
123	## Evidence appendix
124	
125	- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
126	  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
127	  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
128	- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
129	  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
130	- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
131	  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
132	- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
133	- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
134	  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
135	- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
136	  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
137	  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
138	  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).
139	
140	[restricting-domains]: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/organization-policy/restricting-domains
141	[troubleshoot-org-policies]: https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/troubleshoot-org-policies
142

# Credential lifecycle: the Binding-revoke hole (keyfile tier)

*2026-06-04. Surfaced in heat ₣BV while bringing the `blockade` (moriah / airgap)
crucible up on macOS Docker Desktop (branch `bv-macos-crucible-20260603-BVAAE`);
repair tracked under `rbk-08-credential-repairs`. Depot under study: the
canonical standing depot `cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001`, org `247899326218`.*

## Headline

The keyfile credential tier (RBSHR's name for the current model: each role holds
an RBRA key file, possession = identity) **fuses three independent object
lifecycles into one operation**, and never wires the revoke half of one of them.
The result: IAM bindings accumulate without bound, and re-credentialing trips two
separate Google eventual-consistency edges. All of it follows from one structural
hole, stated precisely below. None of it is macOS-specific.

## Objects

- **SA** — a GCP service account, e.g. `director-canest-dir@…`. Identity is
  email **+ uid**; recreating the same name yields a *new* uid.
- **Key** — credential material *on* an SA. Serialized to disk as the **RBRA**
  file the operator holds. (Key = GCP-side; RBRA = its delivery.)
- **Binding** — one `(role → member)` entry; the member *is* an SA; it lives on
  a **Resource**.
- **Resource** — a thing carrying an IAM policy: the **project**, the **GAR
  repo**, the **Mason SA**.

## Three independent lifecycles

1. **SA**: create → exists → delete. *On delete, GCP cannot drop bindings whose
   member vanished — it retags them `deleted:…?uid=` and keeps them.*
2. **Key**: add → valid → delete. *Deleting a Key is a blunt kill of one
   credential; the SA is untouched.*
3. **Binding**: grant (add member) → active → **revoke (remove member)**. Keyed
   by member = SA.

Re-credentialing an operator *requires only the Key lifecycle*: add a new Key,
kill the old one.

## The hole

The code fuses "create the identity" with "issue a credential." One invest does
**SA-create + Binding-grant + Key-create** together; its only inverse (divest)
does **SA-delete** alone. From that fusion, two defects:

- **H1 — the Binding lifecycle is half-wired.** `grant` runs at invest;
  **`revoke` is never called anywhere.** Divest deletes the SA and stops
  (`zrbgg_divest_role` never opens an IAM policy). So Bindings are `+1` per
  invest and `0` per divest — monotonic, unbounded.
- **H2 — re-key invokes the wrong lifecycle.** "Re-key" runs the **SA**
  lifecycle (delete + recreate — chosen as a blunt guarantee the old Key is
  dead) when it only needed the **Key** lifecycle. So the SA *and its Bindings*
  churn every issuance, when only the Key had to.

## The three faces (all consequences of the hole)

- **Binding leak ← H1.** Bindings granted, never revoked, accumulate as
  `deleted:` ghosts on every Resource. Observed: **project 145** (69 director,
  46 retriever, 30 governor-owner), **GAR repo 23**, **Mason SA 23**. *Benign so
  far — Experiment A (below) shows the 145-ghost policy still resubmits cleanly —
  but it marches toward GCP's ~1500-member allow-policy cap independent of any
  org policy.* A comment in `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` already records the
  ghosts as a known, "accepted" cost; they have now become unbounded.
- **create→grant race ← H2.** Each cycle mints a *fresh* SA and immediately
  grants it. The org enforces Domain Restricted Sharing
  (`iam.allowedPolicyMemberDomains`, allow-listing customer `C02ystilf` =
  scaleinvariant.org); a brand-new SA is not yet recognized as in-customer, so
  the grant is rejected (HTTP 400, "incompatible with the provided policy"), and
  `rbgi`'s retry tolerance does **not** cover that error — so it dies on the
  first try. *This blocked `blockade`.* Google documents the propagation lag
  (≈2 min, up to 7+). [restricting-domains], [troubleshoot-org-policies]
- **delete→recreate race ← H2.** Divest deletes the SA and reports it gone, but
  the immediate re-create's preflight still sees it ("already exists"). *This
  blocked `dogfight`* the next morning. A state check minutes later showed the SA
  fully gone with no soft-delete reservation — the create-preflight simply read
  a not-yet-converged view.

Both races are **intermittent and host-independent** (server-side); they pass
routinely on cerebro/Cygwin and lost on macOS two days running. The macOS
crucible itself is sound — `siege` is 60/60 green there.

## The fix — separate the lifecycles

Stop fusing. Split the one invest into two operations:

- **Establish** (one-time per SA — at depot setup / operator enrollment):
  ensure the SA exists and **grant its Bindings once**. Idempotent — a no-op when
  already established.
- **Re-key** (every issuance): **rotate the Key only** — delete all existing
  Keys (the blunt kill, preserved), add one. Never touches the SA or its
  Bindings.
- **Teardown** (rare — genuine retirement): the missing inverse — **revoke
  Bindings, then delete the SA** (H1's never-wired half), run once at end-of-life
  rather than every cycle.

This closes H1 (Bindings granted once, revoked on real teardown — never churned)
and H2 (re-key is Key-only; the SA never gets recreated). Scope notes:

1. It's a refactor, not a line: one fused verb → establish / re-key (+ a real
   teardown).
2. Establish must be **idempotent** ("ensure," not "create").
3. Re-key **presumes established** (assert SA + Bindings present, else establish).
4. Blunt-kill preserved: re-key = delete *all* Keys, add one.
5. The create→grant DRS race doesn't vanish — it **relocates to establish**
   (now ≈once per identity, off the hot path). Keep the DRS-400 in `rbgi`'s
   retry tolerance there as cheap insurance.
6. Existing ghosts (145 + 23 + 23) need a **one-time sweep** — the split stops
   new ones, doesn't clean old. Safe: only `deleted:*` removed (Experiment A
   shows the policy is writable).
7. Cross-host see-saw is **orthogonal** — a shared identity re-keyed bluntly
   invalidates other holders either way; unchanged.
8. **Governor out of scope** (deliberate single-governor, datestamped,
   low-churn simplification). Fix targets director/retriever.

## Footnote: federation

The roadmap's operator-federation tier (RBSHR; `memo-20260527-operator-credential-models.md`)
grants capabilities to stable `principal://` subjects with central revocation —
no per-cycle SA at all, so it would obviate this hole entirely. It is the
**eventual successor tier, not the MVP fix**; the keyfile tier stays and is
repaired in place as above.

## Evidence appendix

- DRS + policy state: `gcloud resource-manager org-policies …`,
  `gcloud projects get-iam-policy cancbhm-d-canest3bhm100001` (payor-token, 06-04).
  DRS allow-list = `C02ystilf`.
- **Experiment A** — `set-iam-policy` of the unchanged 145-ghost project policy
  → HTTP 200. Ghosts do not block grants.
- **Experiment B′** — grant `roles/cloudbuild.builds.editor` to the now-propagated
  director SA → HTTP 200. Freshness was the only variable.
- create→grant race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260603-103842-26773-301.txt`.
- delete→recreate race: `logs-buk/hist-rbw-adI-sh-20260604-092730-7113-822.txt`,
  suite `logs-buk/hist-rbw-ts-dogfight-20260604-092601-3729-496.txt`.
- Code: `rbgi_add_project_iam_role` + the accepted-ghost comment
  (`Tools/rbk/rbgi_IAM.sh`); `zrbgg_divest_role`, `rbgg_invest_director`,
  `rbgg_invest_retriever` (`Tools/rbk/rbgg_Governor.sh`);
  `rbtdrk_role_invest_impl` (`Tools/rbk/rbtd/src/rbtdrk_canonical.rs`).

1	# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation
2	
3	Date: 2026-05-27
4	
5	Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
6	per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
7	organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
8	the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
9	single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
10	roadmap entry.
11	
12	---
13	
14	## The plan
15	
16	RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:
17	
18	- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
19	  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
20	- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
21	  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.
22	
23	A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
24	exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
25	codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
26	requirement, which is also the natural paywall.
27	
28	---
29	
30	## Two models, two tiers
31	
32	| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
33	|---|---|---|
34	| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
35	| GCP organization | not required | required |
36	| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
37	| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
38	| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |
39	
40	Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
41	Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
42	model-blind.
43	
44	---
45	
46	## The organization requirement is the tier boundary
47	
48	Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
49	**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
50	structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.
51	
52	An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
53	creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
54	tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
55	pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
56	verify a domain.
57	
58	---
59	
60	## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products
61	
62	GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
63	authenticating?*
64	
65	| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
66	|---|---|---|
67	| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
68	| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
69	| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |
70	
71	RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
72	actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.
73	
74	---
75	
76	## How federation authenticates
77	
78	The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
79	touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
80	**never runtime**.
81	
82	**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
83	workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
84	issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
85	claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
86	receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
87	`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
88	Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
89	already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
90	roster.
91	
92	**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
93	IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
94	(RFC 8628):
95	
96	1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
97	   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
98	2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
99	   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
100	   is not involved.
101	3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.
102	
103	**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**
104	
105	```
106	POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
107	  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
108	  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
109	  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
110	  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
111	  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
112	```
113	
114	Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
115	no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.
116	
117	**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
118	1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
119	and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
120	long session.
121	
122	---
123	
124	## IdP landscape
125	
126	Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
127	Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
128	practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
129	The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
130	Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
131	IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.
132	
133	---
134	
135	## Refresh-token policy
136	
137	A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
138	Confine it to exactly one place:
139	
140	- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
141	  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
142	- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
143	  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
144	  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
145	  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
146	  token.
147	- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
148	  outside the operator mode enum.
149	
150	A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
151	on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.
152	
153	---
154	
155	## Where configuration lives
156	
157	Three homes, by scope:
158	
159	- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
160	  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
161	  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
162	  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
163	  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
164	  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
165	  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
166	  independently straddle modes.
167	- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
168	  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
169	  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
170	- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
171	  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.
172	
173	There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
174	the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
175	to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
176	secret.
177	
178	---
179	
180	## Operator-admin verbs
181	
182	The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
183	**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
184	operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
185	keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
186	on the operator's `principal://` subject.
187	
188	Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
189	**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
190	trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
191	enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.
192	
193	---
194	
195	## One consumer path, real and synthetic
196	
197	Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
198	resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
199	only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
200	device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh

# Operator Credential Models — Keyfile and Workforce Federation

Date: 2026-05-27

Status: **Committed plan — support two operator-credential models as permanent,
per-depot-selected tiers.** The keyfile tier is the shipping default and needs no
organization. The federation tier is future build; this memo fixes its shape and
the architecture that lets both live in the codebase while never coexisting in a
single depot. It is the source document for the RBSHR "Operator federation"
roadmap entry.

---

## The plan

RBK supports two operator-credential models, permanently, as a free/paid split:

- **Keyfile** — service-account key files on disk (RBRA). No GCP organization
  required. The free, default tier; ships today.
- **Federation** — Workforce Identity Federation against an external IdP. No
  secret on disk. Requires a GCP organization. The paid tier.

A single per-depot **mode enum** selects which model is in force. One depot runs
exactly one model — the two never coexist within an installation — but the
codebase carries both forever. The boundary between the tiers is the organization
requirement, which is also the natural paywall.

---

## Two models, two tiers

| | **Keyfile** (free) | **Federation** (paid) |
|---|---|---|
| Operator secret on disk | SA key (RBRA) | none |
| GCP organization | not required | required |
| Identity proof | possession of the key | fresh IdP sign-in |
| Revocation | rotate / delete the key | disable at the IdP |
| Roles (gov/dir/ret) | one SA key file each | IAM grants on the operator's principal |

Both models terminate in the same artifact: a short-lived Google access token.
Everything downstream of the token — every depot API call — is identical and
model-blind.

---

## The organization requirement is the tier boundary

Federation's trust setting — the **workforce pool** — can only be created at the
**organization** level; a plain project cannot hold one. So federation is
structurally org-only, and that requirement is the line between free and paid.

An organization is obtainable at no cost via **Cloud Identity Free**, which
creates the org once the owner **verifies ownership of a DNS domain**. So the paid
tier's real prerequisite is "the operator controls a domain," not "the operator
pays Google." The keyfile tier exists to serve operators who cannot or will not
verify a domain.

---

## Workforce vs Workload — two federation products

GCP has two federation products answering one question: *what kind of identity is
authenticating?*

| | **Workforce** Identity Federation | **Workload** Identity Federation |
|---|---|---|
| For | **Humans** (console, gcloud, API) | **Machines** (CI, apps, cross-cloud) |
| Pool scope | **Organization-level** | Project-level |
| RBK use | **Operator federation** (this memo) | The **envoy** — GCP→AWS egress |

RBK uses both, for different actors; they coexist but never chain for a single
actor. The org-level pool scope is why operator federation is org-only.

---

## How federation authenticates

The flow is two legs, both inside RBK's existing curl/openssl/jq stack. gcloud
touches **setup only** (pool/provider creation, itself REST- and console-doable),
**never runtime**.

**Setup (once, Payor-side).** The Payor — as org admin — creates an org-level
workforce pool plus a provider trusting an external IdP (any OIDC or SAML 2.0
issuer). An **attribute mapping** pins `google.subject` to an *immutable* IdP
claim (e.g. `sub`/`oid`, ≤127 bytes); a mutable claim invites spoofing. Operators
receive depot capabilities as IAM grants on a **federated principal**:
`principal://iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/subject/SUBJECT`.
Identity proofing lives entirely in the IdP; RBK only grants capabilities to an
already-known principal, and the depot remains the home of the role-to-identity
roster.

**Leg 1 — IdP auth (outside Google).** Obtain an OIDC **ID token (JWT)** from the
IdP. The headless realization is the OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant
(RFC 8628):

1. The tool POSTs the IdP's device-authorization endpoint → `user_code`,
   `verification_uri`, `device_code`.
2. The tool prints "go to `<uri>` and enter `WXYZ-1234`." The human authorizes in
   **any** browser, on any device — proving themselves to *their own* IdP. Google
   is not involved.
3. The tool polls the IdP's token endpoint until it returns the ID token.

**Leg 2 — STS exchange (pure REST against `sts.googleapis.com`):**

```
POST https://sts.googleapis.com/v1/token        # send NO Authorization header
  grant_type         = urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:token-exchange   # RFC 8693
  audience           = //iam.googleapis.com/locations/global/workforcePools/POOL/providers/PROVIDER
  subject_token      = <IdP ID token from Leg 1>
  subject_token_type = urn:ietf:params:oauth:token-type:id_token
  → returns a short-lived Google access_token
```

Then bearer that token (`Authorization: Bearer …`) against any GCP API. Leg 2 has
no signing — it just POSTs a token RBK already holds.

**Session cap.** A Workforce session is configurable **15 min – 12 h, default
1 h** — a hard GCP ceiling. Long-running operation comes from re-running Leg 2,
and, where unattended, from a refresh token (test rig only) — never from a single
long session.

---

## IdP landscape

Federation trusts any **OIDC or SAML 2.0** issuer — Entra ID, Okta, AD FS, Ping,
Auth0, self-hosted Keycloak/Dex, or GCP Identity Platform as an in-house IdP. In
practice each operator org uses its existing IdP; RBK only points the pool at it.
The headless device flow (RFC 8628) is supported across Entra, Okta, Auth0,
Keycloak, and Google, so Leg 1 is curl-friendly on essentially every realistic
IdP. The only install where RBK itself chooses the IdP is the project's test org.

---

## Refresh-token policy

A persisted refresh token is the one durable secret federation can reintroduce.
Confine it to exactly one place:

- **Shipped human operators: no persisted refresh token.** Device flow per
  session; reauthentication on the operator org's own cadence.
- **Synthetic-human test rig (the project's own test org only): one persisted
  refresh token per firewalled machine** — the single acknowledged durable-secret
  residual, re-exchanged headless within the session cap. Issuing it requires the
  **`offline_access` scope**; without that scope most IdPs return no refresh
  token.
- **Payor: keeps its own OAuth refresh token** — the bootstrap human identity,
  outside the operator mode enum.

A persisted refresh token anywhere in the shipped path would recreate the
on-disk-secret problem federation exists to remove. Keep it caged in the test rig.

---

## Where configuration lives

Three homes, by scope:

- **Depot regime (RBRD)** holds the **mode enum** and, in federation mode, the
  org-wide federation facts: **pool, provider, audience**. These are identical for
  every operator of the depot. RBRD is tripwire-protected (`rbw-rdc` checks local
  `rbrd.env` against the GAR-inscribed copy), so the mode is **tamper-evident**: a
  depot cannot be silently downgraded from federation to keyfile without a
  deliberate re-inscription. Placing the enum here also makes one-mode-per-depot a
  structural fact rather than a per-machine convention — operators cannot
  independently straddle modes.
- **Station scope (or local)** holds only the per-operator **credential source** —
  how *this* machine obtains its IdP token (an interactive session cache, or, for
  the test rig, an executable backed by the caged refresh token).
- **RBRA files exist only in keyfile mode** — one per role
  (governor/director/retriever). In federation mode there are none.

There is no separate per-operator credential file: org-wide facts live in RBRD,
the one machine-local pointer lives at station scope, and nothing in between needs
to persist. A leaked station credential-source pointer is inert — it holds no
secret.

---

## Operator-admin verbs

The verbs — **mantle** (Payor→Governor), **invest** (Governor→Director/Retriever),
**divest**, **roster** — keep their names, actors, and directions in both models;
operator vocabulary does not change with the tier. Only their bodies differ:
keyfile mints an SA and downloads a key; federation adds or removes an IAM binding
on the operator's `principal://` subject.

Federation adds exactly one verb keyfile has no equivalent of: a one-time
**Payor-side federation-setup** verb that configures the workforce pool and IdP
trust. The test-rig "log a machine in for a month" is a separate **test-only
enrollment** verb, never in the shipped register.

---

## One consumer path, real and synthetic

Real and synthetic operators walk the **same consumer path** — read config,
resolve a token, call GCP, never knowing what is behind the token. They differ
only in the credential source: a real human's resolves against an interactive
device-flow session; the synthetic human's resolves against the caged refresh
token. This sameness is required, not convenient: CI must exercise the same code
path a real operator does, or it validates the wrong thing.

---

## The single code seam

Both models meet at one function: a **role-keyed token accessor** ("give me a
token as director"), with the mode enum branching inside it —

- **keyfile** → resolve role → RBRA file → signed-JWT exchange;
- **federation** → device flow → STS exchange.

Downstream is already model-blind: every GCP call takes a bare token string and
adds `Authorization: Bearer`, with no knowledge of its origin.

Today the keyfile token function is keyed by **file path**, and ~30 call sites
pass an explicit RBRA file — so the keyfile concept leaks into every consumer. The
enabling step, shippable now with **no behavior change**, is to collapse those
call sites behind the role-keyed accessor. After that refactor, federation is a
second branch inside one function; the call sites and the HTTP layer never change
again.

**Sequencing: refactor to the role accessor first (keyfile-only, today), then add
the federation branch.**

---

## Open at implementation time

- RBRD field names and any new mints for the mode enum and federation facts.
- Concrete credential-source wiring for the interactive human (device-flow output
  → what the token resolver consumes).
- The chosen IdP(s) for the test org and the federation-setup verb's exact API
  calls.

---

## Sources

- [Workforce Identity Federation — overview](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-identity-federation)
- [Configure Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/configuring-workforce-identity-federation) — pools, providers, attribute mapping, session duration (15 min – 12 h, default 1 h)
- [Obtain short-lived tokens for Workforce Identity Federation](https://docs.cloud.google.com/iam/docs/workforce-obtaining-short-lived-credentials) — STS token-exchange, file- and executable-sourced credentials
- [Using Workforce Identity Federation with API-based web applications](https://cloud.google.com/blog/products/identity-security/using-workforce-identity-federation-with-api-based-web-applications/) — gcloud-free runtime; IdP-auth then raw STS POST
- [Cloud Identity editions](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/editions) and [set up Cloud Identity as admin](https://docs.cloud.google.com/identity/docs/how-to/set-up-cloud-identity-admin) — free org via domain verification
- [RFC 8628 — OAuth 2.0 Device Authorization Grant](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc8628)

1	#!/bin/bash
2	#
3	# Copyright 2025 Scale Invariant, Inc.
4	#
5	# Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
6	# you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
7	# You may obtain a copy of the License at

12	# distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
13	# WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
14	# See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
15	# limitations under the License.
16	#
17	# Author: Brad Hyslop <bhyslop@scaleinvariant.org>
18	#
19	# Recipe Bottle Auth - RBRA/RBRO credential load and role token mint
20	
21	set -euo pipefail
22	
23	# Multiple inclusion detection
24	test -z "${ZRBA_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
25	ZRBA_SOURCED=1
26	
27	######################################################################
28	# Internal Functions (zrba_*)
29	
30	zrba_kindle() {
31	  test -z "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba already kindled"
32	
33	  # Ensure dependency kindled first (rba mints tokens via rbgo)
34	  zrbgo_sentinel
35	
36	  readonly ZRBA_KINDLED=1
37	}
38	
39	zrba_sentinel() {
40	  test "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rba not kindled - call zrba_kindle first"
41	}
42	
43	######################################################################
44	# External / RBTOE Pattern Functions
45	
46	rba_get_governor_token_capture() {
47	  zrba_sentinel
48	
49	  # Need access to RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE from regime
50	  test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}" || return 1
51	
52	  local z_token
53	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}") || return 1
54	
55	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
56	  echo    "${z_token}"
57	}
58	
59	rba_authenticate_role_capture() {
60	  zrba_sentinel
61	
62	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
63	
64	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
65	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
66	
67	  buc_log_args "Authenticating with RBRA file: ${z_rbra_file}"
68	
69	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
70	
71	  local z_token
72	  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || return 1
73	
74	  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
75	
76	  echo "${z_token}"
77	}
78	
79	rba_extract_json_to_rbra() {
80	  zrba_sentinel
81	
82	  local -r z_json_path="$1"
83	  local -r z_rbra_path="$2"
84	  local -r z_lifetime_sec="$3"
85	  local -r z_expected_project_id="${4:-}"
86	
87	  test -f "${z_json_path}" || buc_die "Service account JSON not found: ${z_json_path}"
88	
89	  buc_info "Extracting service account credentials from JSON"
90	
91	  buc_log_args 'Extract fields'
92	  local z_client_email
93	  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_json_path}") \
94	                                        || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
95	  test -n "${z_client_email}"           || buc_die "Empty client_email in JSON"
96	  test    "${z_client_email}" != "null" || buc_die "Null client_email in JSON"
97	
98	  local z_private_key
99	  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_json_path}") \
100	                                       || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
101	  test -n "${z_private_key}"           || buc_die "Empty private_key in JSON"
102	  test    "${z_private_key}" != "null" || buc_die "Null private_key in JSON"
103	
104	  local z_project_id
105	  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_json_path}") \
106	                                      || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
107	  test -n "${z_project_id}"           || buc_die "Empty project_id in JSON"
108	  test    "${z_project_id}" != "null" || buc_die "Null project_id in JSON"
109	
110	  if test -n "${z_expected_project_id}"; then
111	    buc_log_args "Verify project matches expected: ${z_expected_project_id}"
112	    test "${z_project_id}" = "${z_expected_project_id}" \
113	      || buc_die "Project mismatch: JSON has '${z_project_id}', expected '${z_expected_project_id}'"
114	  else
115	    buc_log_args "No project validation - accepting JSON project_id: ${z_project_id}"
116	  fi
117	
118	  buc_log_args 'Write RBRA file'
119	  # CAUTION: jq -r unescapes JSON \n to real newlines, so z_private_key holds a
120	  # multi-line PEM string. printf '%s' below preserves real newlines into the
121	  # RBRA file. Consumer (rbgo_OAuth.sh:zrbgo_build_jwt_capture) tolerates either
122	  # real-newline or '\n'-escape form via printf '%b'; do not "normalize" to one
123	  # form without auditing the consumer.
124	  {
125	    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
126	    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
127	    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
128	    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=%s\n'  "${z_lifetime_sec}"
129	  } > "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_path}"
130	
131	  test -f "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file: ${z_rbra_path}"
132	
133	  buc_warn "Consider deleting source JSON after verification: ${z_json_path}"
134	}
135	
136	# RBTOE: RBRA Load Pattern
137	# Sources an RBRA file and validates required fields
138	rba_rbra_load() {
139	  zrba_sentinel
140	
141	  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"
142	
143	  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file path required"
144	  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file not found: ${z_rbra_file}"
145	
146	  buc_log_args "Loading and validating RBRA credentials from ${z_rbra_file}"
147	
148	  # Source the RBRA file
149	  source "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: failed to source RBRA file"
150	
151	  # Validate required fields
152	  test -n "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
153	  test -n "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
154	  test -n "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
155	
156	  # Check for null values
157	  test "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
158	  test "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
159	  test "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
160	
161	  buc_log_args "RBRA validation successful: ${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL} in project ${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}"
162	}
163	
164	# RBTOE: RBRO Load Pattern
165	# Thin wrapper: defensively sources rbro_regime.sh (callers don't need to know
166	# its path, which moved under AAD's payor/ subdirectory migration), then
167	# delegates to rbro_load. Parallels rba_rbra_load at the call-signature level
168	# even though that function carries its own validation; the uniform rba_*
169	# load-through-utility convention is the load-bearing reason this wrapper exists.
170	rba_rbro_load() {
171	  zrba_sentinel
172	
173	  buc_log_args "Loading RBRO OAuth credentials"
174	
175	  # Source regime module if not already loaded
176	  if test -z "${ZRBRO_SOURCED:-}"; then
177	    source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbro_regime.sh"
178	  fi
179	
180	  # Delegate to regime's canonical load
181	  rbro_load
182	
183	  buc_log_args "RBRO validation successful"
184	}
185	
186	# eof

#
# Recipe Bottle Auth - RBRA/RBRO credential load and role token mint

set -euo pipefail

# Multiple inclusion detection
test -z "${ZRBA_SOURCED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba multiply sourced - check sourcing hierarchy"
ZRBA_SOURCED=1

######################################################################
# Internal Functions (zrba_*)

zrba_kindle() {
  test -z "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" || buc_die "Module rba already kindled"

  # Ensure dependency kindled first (rba mints tokens via rbgo)
  zrbgo_sentinel

  readonly ZRBA_KINDLED=1
}

zrba_sentinel() {
  test "${ZRBA_KINDLED:-}" = "1" || buc_die "Module rba not kindled - call zrba_kindle first"
}

######################################################################
# External / RBTOE Pattern Functions

rba_get_governor_token_capture() {
  zrba_sentinel

  # Need access to RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE from regime
  test -n "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE:-}" || return 1

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${RBDC_GOVERNOR_RBRA_FILE}") || return 1

  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1
  echo    "${z_token}"
}

rba_authenticate_role_capture() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"

  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1

  buc_log_args "Authenticating with RBRA file: ${z_rbra_file}"

  source "${z_rbra_file}" || return 1

  local z_token
  z_token=$(rbgo_get_token_capture "${z_rbra_file}") || return 1

  test -n "${z_token}" || return 1

  echo "${z_token}"
}

rba_extract_json_to_rbra() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_json_path="$1"
  local -r z_rbra_path="$2"
  local -r z_lifetime_sec="$3"
  local -r z_expected_project_id="${4:-}"

  test -f "${z_json_path}" || buc_die "Service account JSON not found: ${z_json_path}"

  buc_info "Extracting service account credentials from JSON"

  buc_log_args 'Extract fields'
  local z_client_email
  z_client_email=$(jq -r '.client_email' "${z_json_path}") \
                                        || buc_die "Failed to extract client_email"
  test -n "${z_client_email}"           || buc_die "Empty client_email in JSON"
  test    "${z_client_email}" != "null" || buc_die "Null client_email in JSON"

  local z_private_key
  z_private_key=$(jq -r '.private_key' "${z_json_path}") \
                                       || buc_die "Failed to extract private_key"
  test -n "${z_private_key}"           || buc_die "Empty private_key in JSON"
  test    "${z_private_key}" != "null" || buc_die "Null private_key in JSON"

  local z_project_id
  z_project_id=$(jq -r '.project_id' "${z_json_path}") \
                                      || buc_die "Failed to extract project_id"
  test -n "${z_project_id}"           || buc_die "Empty project_id in JSON"
  test    "${z_project_id}" != "null" || buc_die "Null project_id in JSON"

  if test -n "${z_expected_project_id}"; then
    buc_log_args "Verify project matches expected: ${z_expected_project_id}"
    test "${z_project_id}" = "${z_expected_project_id}" \
      || buc_die "Project mismatch: JSON has '${z_project_id}', expected '${z_expected_project_id}'"
  else
    buc_log_args "No project validation - accepting JSON project_id: ${z_project_id}"
  fi

  buc_log_args 'Write RBRA file'
  # CAUTION: jq -r unescapes JSON \n to real newlines, so z_private_key holds a
  # multi-line PEM string. printf '%s' below preserves real newlines into the
  # RBRA file. Consumer (rbgo_OAuth.sh:zrbgo_build_jwt_capture) tolerates either
  # real-newline or '\n'-escape form via printf '%b'; do not "normalize" to one
  # form without auditing the consumer.
  {
    printf 'RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL="%s"\n'      "${z_client_email}"
    printf 'RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY="'; printf '%s' "${z_private_key}"; printf '"\n'
    printf 'RBRA_PROJECT_ID="%s"\n'        "${z_project_id}"
    printf 'RBRA_TOKEN_LIFETIME_SEC=%s\n'  "${z_lifetime_sec}"
  } > "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file ${z_rbra_path}"

  test -f "${z_rbra_path}" || buc_die "Failed to write RBRA file: ${z_rbra_path}"

  buc_warn "Consider deleting source JSON after verification: ${z_json_path}"
}

# RBTOE: RBRA Load Pattern
# Sources an RBRA file and validates required fields
rba_rbra_load() {
  zrba_sentinel

  local -r z_rbra_file="${1}"

  test -n "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file path required"
  test -f "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA file not found: ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "Loading and validating RBRA credentials from ${z_rbra_file}"

  # Source the RBRA file
  source "${z_rbra_file}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: failed to source RBRA file"

  # Validate required fields
  test -n "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
  test -n "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY missing from ${z_rbra_file}"
  test -n "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID:-}" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID missing from ${z_rbra_file}"

  # Check for null values
  test "${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
  test "${RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PRIVATE_KEY is null in ${z_rbra_file}"
  test "${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}" != "null" || buc_die "rba_rbra_load: RBRA_PROJECT_ID is null in ${z_rbra_file}"

  buc_log_args "RBRA validation successful: ${RBRA_CLIENT_EMAIL} in project ${RBRA_PROJECT_ID}"
}

# RBTOE: RBRO Load Pattern
# Thin wrapper: defensively sources rbro_regime.sh (callers don't need to know
# its path, which moved under AAD's payor/ subdirectory migration), then
# delegates to rbro_load. Parallels rba_rbra_load at the call-signature level
# even though that function carries its own validation; the uniform rba_*
# load-through-utility convention is the load-bearing reason this wrapper exists.
rba_rbro_load() {
  zrba_sentinel

  buc_log_args "Loading RBRO OAuth credentials"

  # Source regime module if not already loaded
  if test -z "${ZRBRO_SOURCED:-}"; then
    source "${BASH_SOURCE[0]%/*}/rbro_regime.sh"
  fi

  # Delegate to regime's canonical load
  rbro_load

  buc_log_args "RBRO validation successful"
}

content.text: This is a design-exploration pace, and the source material is rich. Let me verify one thing the docket leans on heavily — whether the lifecycle split (establish/re-key) it names as "the enabler" is actually in place — so I'm discussing from real state, not the memo's plan.

I've got enough to discuss substantively. One thing on the ground worth flagging up front: the governor's public verb surface is **still role-typed and duplicated** — `rbgg_invest_director`/`rbgg_invest_retriever`, `rbgg_divest_director`/`rbgg_divest_retriever`, `rbgg_roster_directors`/`rbgg_roster_retrievers`. The H1 revoke layer landed (`rbgi_revoke_*`), but I don't see public `establish`/`rekey`/`teardown` verbs yet. So the "enabler" the docket leans on isn't fully surfaced — which matters for sequencing, and I'll ask you about it below.

---

**The goal, one sentence:** decide whether to move RBRA from one-SA-per-role-type to one-SA-per-*person* carrying a chosen role-set — and prove least-privilege survives the move, not just that it's tidier. (Silks `consider-per-person-multirole-rbra` fits; no rename.)

Here's how the terrain looks to me after reading both memos and the code.

**The crux is a change in the *class* of the guarantee, not its presence.** Today least-privilege is *structural*: the retriever SA physically lacks director bindings, and `rbgg_invest_retriever` hardcodes the retriever grant-set — there's no per-call choice to get wrong. Per-person replaces that with least-privilege *by declared-and-audited grant-set*: "this person is retriever-only" becomes a declaration the establish verb honors and a roster can read back, rather than an impossibility. That's a strictly weaker guarantee class, and it introduces an error mode that literally cannot happen today — a mis-declared role-set putting director grants on a retriever-only person.

**But — and this is what tilts me toward "do it":** that exact downgrade is *coming anyway* with federation. The federation tier grants capabilities to a `principal://` subject by IAM binding — declared-and-audited is the only model it has. So per-person isn't introducing a foreign weakness; it's pre-adopting federation's guarantee class in the keyfile tier. And the shapes then match: keyfile establish = "grant declared role-set on this person's SA," federation invest = "grant declared role-set on this person's principal" — same verb, one branch differing only in SA-vs-principal. That's the "smoother migration" the docket claims, and it's real architectural payoff, not just vocabulary.

So my honest synthesis: **lean yes, conditional on building the audit half.** The security guarantee survives *if and only if* establish takes an explicit declared role-set and roster/audit diffs actual-grants-on-SA against that declaration. Without the audit half, you've just got an unenforced convention and the downgrade is real. With it, you've got federation's guarantee a tier early.

The one thing that genuinely moves the verdict, and that I'm guessing at:

**Does any operator actually hold both roles today?** The whole capability universe here is tiny — governor's out of scope per the H1/H2 memo, so it's just {director}, {retriever}, {director,retriever}. If nobody is both, per-person buys only the verb-surface collapse and the migration-shape match (both real, but you're re-indexing one-role-per-SA by person instead of by role — modest). If someone *is* both, per-person also collapses two on-disk keys into one — and the least-privilege story there is easy, because two role-keys on one machine were never actually isolated anyway (machine compromise took both regardless).

Two questions for you before I'd commit to a recommendation:

1. **Operator population** — is "one human, both director and retriever" a real case you serve (or expect to), or are these always distinct principals on distinct machines?
2. **Where did the lifecycle split actually land**, and how do you see this pace sequenced against it? If establish/re-key/teardown still has to be built, per-person is cheapest to fold *into* that rewrite (the code's already open) rather than as a second pass — but that only works if the split hasn't fully shipped yet.

Where do you want to push first?

content.text:
Yes — let me actually reason it rather than inherit the memo's "governor out of scope," because I think the memo got the *conclusion* roughly right and the *reason* wrong, and the reason is what matters here.

When I sort the four actors by what kind of thing their credential *is*, they fall into three tiers, and per-person only type-checks for one of them:

**Payor — out by mechanism.** Payor isn't an SA at all. It's a human OAuth identity (RBRO refresh token, GUI consent, Manor/org creation) — the federation memo explicitly holds it "outside the operator mode enum," the bootstrap human at the root of trust. The per-person collapse is "one *SA* carrying a role-set"; payor has no SA to carry. The fact that some payor procedures are scripted (PayorMantlesGovernor, levy depot) doesn't change that — those scripts spend the payor's *OAuth* token to act; scriptability is orthogonal to credential kind. Payor sits *above* the role-set, not within it. Even a solo operator who is personally payor-and-everything-else still holds the payor OAuth credential separately from any SA. So: structurally excluded, not a judgment call.

**Governor — out by role-kind, and this is the load-bearing part.** The memo excluded governor for being "single, datestamped, low-churn" — a *convenience* argument about the lifecycle hole. That's the weak reason. The strong reason is separation of duties: governor is the capability *administrator* — it invests/divests/rosters the very SAs the per-person model manages. Two things follow:

- Per-person doesn't even type-check for it. Governor is a *singleton institutional* role (one per depot), not a per-operator workload role. "Each person's governor capability-set" isn't a thing.
- Folding it into a person's role-set is a real escalation, not a theoretical one — and here's the sharpener: the **director capability is exercised by running foreign build code** (Cloud Build executes the build steps; that's the whole reason RBSFH hygiene exists). A director-only SA that gets compromised through a malicious build can't grant itself anything. A director+governor union SA *can rewrite the depot's IAM from inside a build.* That blast radius is categorically worse than "two keys on one disk," because the disk argument only concedes credential theft, whereas this concedes self-escalation by code the director role is *designed to run*.

So governor's exclusion isn't "it's low-churn so don't bother" — it's "the admin role must never be a member of a workload role-set, by construction." That's a design *constraint* the per-person model has to enforce, not merely audit.

**Director / Retriever — the actual scope.** Both are workload consumers (build, pull). Combining them widens blast radius only *within* the workload tier (a compromised build could also pull any vouched image) — bounded, and the holder would carry both keys on one disk today anyway. That's the case where per-person genuinely improves things: two secrets become one, and the least-privilege story holds because the isolation was already fictional on a shared machine.

So my opinion refines your docket's question: the declarable role-set universe is **exactly {director, retriever}**, governor is *forbidden* from it (not audited into it), and payor is a different mechanism entirely. That's cleaner than "show least-privilege survives" — it's "least-privilege survives for the workload collapse, and the admin/apex roles are kept out by structure."

The one thing I want from you before I bank this: you said governor creation "might differ in this system." I'm reasoning from payor→mantle→governor→invest→director/retriever as the hierarchy. What are you seeing in the governor-creation path that made you flag it? If there's a deployment shape where you actually *want* governor and director to be one principal, I'd want to hear it — because right now I think that specific union is the one combination we should refuse.

lastPrompt: Hmm.  I 'cheated' governor not for structural reasons, but because I didn't want massive 'cult verb' proliferation.  I urge you to read the Federation concept now, in RBS*.adoc documents- it is where…

content.text:
That grounds it — and it flips my position. Two confirmations from the code: `rbgp_governor_mantle()` is literally "Governor SA reset (writes RBRA)" — *create-SA + grant + write-key*, the exact shape as invest, just run by the payor actor against the governor target. And there's "no standalone demantle verb" — the same half-wired asymmetry as invest/no-divest (H1). So mantle isn't a different *kind* of thing; it's `provision(role=governor)` one rung up.

Here's my reassessment, including where I was wrong.

**What I got wrong, named precisely.** I had a real security observation — governor+director on one principal is an escalation, sharpened because the director capability is *exercised by running foreign build code*, so a union SA lets a malicious build rewrite depot IAM. But I let that observation drive me to a *verb-level structural wall* ("governor must be forbidden from the role-set by construction"). That instinct — encode the security distinction as a structural difference in the verb surface — **is exactly the role-specific special-casing that proliferates verbs**, the cost you're killing. The observation is valid; the layer I put it in was wrong.

**The convergence target, now that I've read it.** Both 0527 and RBSHR already commit to *same verbs, bodies branch on mode*. The natural converged shape is `grant/revoke role R on principal P` with **role as a parameter**, not `invest_director` / `invest_retriever` / `mantle` as distinct verbs. Once role is a parameter, **role-count stops driving verb-count** — and that dissolves the whole reason to "cheat" governor out. You excluded it to avoid proliferation; the deeper fix is that the parameterized verb makes including it free.

**Governor: IN.** Fold it into the uniform surface. `mantle` becomes `provision(role=governor)`, run by the payor rung. That *removes* a special-case verb rather than adding one. The role universe becomes `{governor, director, retriever, …}` and the verb surface is just grant / revoke / roster. Your cheat was pointing the right way; it just didn't go far enough.

**Where my security concern goes now — it relocates, it doesn't die.** governor+director on one principal is still genuinely dangerous. But in the converged model that's a *membership fact*, visible in roster/audit, a deliberate depot-owner choice — not a verb-level prohibition. And that's strictly *more* federation-consistent: federation is "IAM grants on principals," where separation-of-duties is always a policy-over-grants question, never a verb shape. So the right home for my concern is "roster surfaces a principal's full role-set so a dangerous union is deliberate and auditable," with maybe an advisory guard — never a structural wall. Convergence and that safety property are orthogonal, which is the thing I missed.

**Payor: still OUT — but reassessed reason, not my security one.** Payor is the OAuth bootstrap *root*: it establishes the trust the whole group model runs inside, it's "outside the mode enum" by the 0527 commitment, and it structurally *cannot* be a federated principal (the identity that configures the workforce pool can't depend on the pool — chicken-and-egg). So payor-as-*subject* stays out of the role-group surface. But payor-as-*actor* performing mantle **is** an instance of the unified grant verb — it's just the top rung that's authorized to `provision(role=governor)`. What stays uniquely payor is the bootstrap/GUI establishment (Manor, OAuth consent, pool setup), which genuinely has no grant-verb analog.

So the converged picture I now hold:

- **Actors are a rung ladder** — payor (OAuth root) → governor → director/retriever — and each rung provisions the rung below *via the same grant verb*. The actor-rung sets authorization; the role parameter sets what's granted.
- **Subjects are the role universe** `{governor, director, retriever, …}`; payor is the only identity that's actor-never-subject (the root).
- **Per-person** = a person is a principal; their capability-set is their role-group memberships; keyfile realization is one SA + one key ("file per person") whose bindings are the union of their roles; federation realization is a `principal://` with the same grants. Same verbs both tiers.

And a bonus that falls out: the half-wired inverse (mantle/no-demantle ≡ invest/no-divest = H1) gets fixed **once, generically** — `revoke role R` / teardown-principal for all roles including governor — instead of per-role. The convergence and the H1 closure are the same refactor.

Two forks for you:

1. **Named verbs vs one implementation.** Your equestrian register (mantles, charters, knights, forfeits) is load-bearing UX vocabulary. Convergence at the *implementation* layer doesn't have to erase the *named* frontispieces — one `provision` core, several named surfaces. Do you want the named verbs preserved as thin aliases over the parameterized core, or do you want the vocabulary itself to collapse to grant/revoke?

2. **Payor's bootstrap edge.** I'm reading 0527+RBSHR as *committing* payor to stay OAuth-outside-the-enum in both tiers. Is that firm, or are you contemplating a more radical all-federation world where even the depot owner is a federated principal and payor's specialness shrinks to just "the first grant"?

message.content: I hadn't necessarily thought through the 'role ladder' idea, seems not a great idea to bake in that hierarchy and instead better to have group membership model.  I for example haven't thought through federation enough to know whether we need to keep a per depot list of members, and their membership in each group.  That seems reasonable, and directly reusable with a refactored RBRA concept along same lines.  My gut is still saying payor is one of these roles since we have bash functions that only payors can do..  So the verbs then just become 'add/remove person' and 'grant/revoke <<role>> for <<persion>>'

content.text:
Good — you're right on both, and the second one corrects me. Let me take them in turn.

**The ladder: conceded, and it shouldn't exist as structure.** What I called a rung ladder is really just an *authorization policy* on the grant verb — "to grant role R you must already hold role R′" (mantle-governor needs payor, invest-director needs governor). That's a policy, and in practice it may not even be RBK's to enforce: in keyfile mode GCP already rejects the grant if your token lacks `serviceAccountAdmin`, so "who can grant what" largely reduces to "what does your credential's IAM allow." So: keep the structure **flat** — people, groups, memberships — and treat grant-authorization as policy (data-driven or just GCP-enforced), never as a baked hierarchy. You're right; I baked in a relationship that should stay emergent.

**Payor as a role: you've got it, and I was over-absolute.** I was conflating two things that should be split:

- **Credential backing / identity lifecycle** — here payor *is* genuinely different: it authenticates by OAuth (RBRO, human consent), not an SA key and not a federated `principal://`. So the *add-person* and *rekey* verbs have a payor-specific body (its "add" is PayorInstall/Establish; its "rekey" is OAuth refresh; there's no SA to mint or key to rotate). This is the same kind of branch the worker roles already have between keyfile-SA and federation-principal — payor is just a third backing.
- **Role membership** — here payor is fully uniform. "Who can do payor things" = "who holds the payor role," managed by the same grant/revoke as every other role. The payor-only bash functions are exactly the evidence: they're gated on holding the payor capability, which is a membership fact.

So the kernel I keep from my earlier resistance is narrow and real: payor's *backing* is OAuth and its *first instance* is a self-bootstrap (PayorInstall — the one irreducible out-of-band seam, like every system's first admin). Everything else about payor is just another row in the membership model. Your verb pair holds:

- `add person P` / `remove person P` — identity lifecycle; **body branches on backing** (SA-mint for keyfile workers, federated-principal register for federation workers, OAuth-install for payor). Uniform *name*, three-way body — worth being honest that this isn't one code path.
- `grant role R for P` / `revoke role R for P` — **fully uniform**, all roles including payor and governor.
- plus `rekey P` — the one genuine outlier: keyfile-worker-only (rotate the SA key). Payor's analog is OAuth refresh; federation has none. Don't assume this one converges.
- plus `roster` — read the member→role map.

And that maps onto the H1/H2 split exactly: `add person` = establish, `grant`/`revoke` = the Binding lifecycle (revoke being H1's never-wired half, now closed *generically* for all roles), `remove person` = teardown, `rekey` = the Key lifecycle alone. The convergence and the H1 closure are one refactor.

**On the per-depot member+membership list — this is the one I think is genuinely open, and you flagged it.** Two sub-shapes, and the choice is a federation question you said you haven't fully worked:

1. **Derive actual membership from IAM** — read the bindings off each person's SA (keyfile) or off the project/resource policy (federation). Can't drift, can't lie. This is what the *current* roster does, except today it reads SA *names* (`director-*`) instead of bindings — and reading bindings is what unlocks multi-role (one person's SA carrying {director, retriever}) without naming hacks.
2. **Store an explicit depot-side roster** — a member list with intended memberships, reconciled against IAM.

My lean: **derive actual from IAM (the can't-lie source), and additionally store an *intended* roster for legibility and audit** — because federation's `principal://...subject/OPAQUE` needs a human-name mapping somewhere, and the intended-vs-actual diff is exactly where your least-privilege guarantee now lives (the roster audit catches "this person was granted a role nobody intended"). That's the same declared-vs-actual shape, lifted to the depot roster. It's directly reusable across the RBRA refactor and federation, as you said — the roster structure is identical; only "what backs a person" differs by mode.

Which leaves the **one decision still genuinely owed**, and it's where my original security worry actually belongs now: the **grant-authorization model** — when someone runs `grant governor for P` or `grant director for P`, what's allowed to gate it? Pure GCP-IAM enforcement (your token either can or can't), or an RBK-side guardrail that flags dangerous unions (the governor+director-on-one-person case — still real, because director runs foreign build code)? That's the load-bearing call, and it's yours. Want to push on that next, or pin down the roster shape (derived vs derived+intended) first?

content.text:
Short answer: the part you've got right is that protecting the file by bucket-IAM (governor writes, everyone reads) is the *same* access mechanism in both models — that's real convergence, the file is mode-agnostic.

Where I think it oversimplifies: **a file that says what each role may do doesn't enforce anything.** A director reading "directors can't push to GAR" is on the honor system — what actually stops them is the IAM grant on their SA, not the file. So the file either:

- **describes** permissions (then it can drift from real IAM — two sources of truth), or
- **drives** them (governor edits the file → it gets applied as the actual IAM grants).

The second is the real convergence point: "governor edits the file" *is* "governor re-grants capabilities," identically whether the principal is an SA (RBSA) or a federated subject (federation).

One question to keep it simple: do you picture that file as the **source that drives the grants**, or as a **description we check against**?

message.content: I definitely can't have role boundaries that are hackable by malicious users.

content.text:
Right — and that settles the fork: it has to **drive** the grants, never describe them.

The reason is exactly your constraint. Any boundary RBK enforces by *reading a file and deciding* is hackable — a malicious director just doesn't run RBK, or runs a patched copy. The only boundary a malicious user can't get around is one enforced **server-side by Google IAM**: a director's token simply can't call an API its grants don't allow, no matter what client it uses.

So:

- **Real boundary** = the IAM grants on the SA (RBSA) or principal (federation). Un-hackable, because Google enforces it, not us. Same substrate in both models — that's your convergence.
- **The file** = at most governor's *declaration* that gets applied *into* IAM. It never enforces at runtime; it's an input, not a gate.

The honor-system version is off the table. Does that match your intent?

message.content: I don't require the file, I do require the behavior.  Should we drop the file concept?  Can we simplify the overall system by refactoring RBRA to be 'identity' based as if the federation pieces were in place?

content.text:
Yes to both.

**Drop the file.** The IAM policy *already is* the role definition and the roster — you can query "what can this identity do" and "who has this role" straight from GCP, and it's server-enforced. A separate file would just be a second source of truth that can drift from the real one. It adds nothing the behavior needs.

**Refactor RBRA to identity-based — yes, that's the simplification.** A person becomes an *identity* whose capabilities are its IAM grants, instead of a role baked into a filename (`director-*`). The keyfile stops meaning "which role" and starts meaning only "how this identity authenticates" — an SA-signed JWT, sitting in exactly the spot a federated token will later occupy. Verbs converge to add/remove identity + grant/revoke role.

The one honest residual: identity, capabilities, and verbs converge *now*; the only thing left mode-specific is the **credential backing** — an SA key on disk vs a secretless federated token. That on-disk secret is precisely what the federation tier removes later. So you converge everything except the secret, and federation becomes a clean drop-in that swaps the backing without touching the identity model.

Want to pin down what an "identity" concretely is in keyfile mode next?
